# Jarvy — LLM Reference

> Jarvy is a fast, Rust-based CLI that standardizes and automates local development environment setup across macOS, Linux, and Windows using a declarative `jarvy.toml` config file.

## Q&A

Q: What is Jarvy?
A: Jarvy is an open-source, cross-platform CLI tool that provisions developer workstations from a single `jarvy.toml` configuration file. It installs tools using native package managers (Homebrew, apt, winget) and ensures every team member has an identical development environment. It replaces manual setup guides, remote dev environments like GitHub Codespaces, and ad-hoc provisioning scripts.

Q: How do I install Jarvy?
A: Five options. (1) Official installer: `curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Cliftonz/jarvy/main/dist/scripts/install.sh | bash` (macOS/Linux) or `irm https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Cliftonz/jarvy/main/dist/scripts/install.ps1 | iex` (Windows PowerShell). (2) Cargo: `cargo install jarvy`. (3) Homebrew: `brew install jarvy`. (4) Binary from https://github.com/Cliftonz/jarvy/releases. (5) For a repo that already ships a `jarvy.toml`, the canonical one-command pattern is to drop `scripts/bootstrap.sh` (https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Cliftonz/jarvy/main/scripts/bootstrap.sh) into the repo so contributors run `./scripts/bootstrap.sh` — installs Jarvy if missing, then runs `jarvy setup`. Idempotent; flags `--no-setup`, `--channel <stable|beta|nightly>`, plus passthrough args to `jarvy setup`.

Q: How do I integrate or implement Jarvy into an existing project?
A: Eight-step workflow. (1) Confirm `jarvy --version` works on the user's machine; do not auto-install — Jarvy runs with elevated privileges, the user must opt in. (2) Detect the project's stack from lockfiles and manifests (e.g. `pnpm-lock.yaml` → `node-pnpm` template, `uv.lock` → `python-uv`, `Cargo.toml` with `[workspace]` → `rust-workspace`). Full decision table at https://jarvy.dev/templates-index/#decision-table. (3) `curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Cliftonz/jarvy/main/examples/<template>/jarvy.toml -o jarvy.toml`. Optionally also fetch the project `Makefile` + `scripts/bootstrap.sh` so new contributors get a one-command setup. (4) Customize `jarvy.toml` to match the project's actual versions from `.nvmrc` / `.python-version` / `go.mod` / `rust-toolchain.toml` / `.tool-versions`; never invent versions. (5) Validate: `jarvy validate && jarvy diff && jarvy setup --dry-run`. (6) Surface the dry-run plan to the user and get explicit confirmation before any real `jarvy setup`. (7) Commit `jarvy.toml` (and the optional `Makefile` + `scripts/bootstrap.sh`). (8) Optionally generate a CI snippet with `jarvy ci-config <provider>` so dev/CI parity is enforced. Full walkthrough at https://jarvy.dev/for-ai-agents/#integration-quickstart-integrate-jarvy-into-this-project.

Q: What problem does Jarvy solve?
A: Jarvy eliminates the "works on my machine" problem. It codifies your team's required tools and versions in a version-controlled config file, so new developers can run `jarvy setup` and have a fully configured workstation in seconds instead of days.

Q: How is Jarvy different from GitHub Codespaces or DevPod?
A: Jarvy provisions your local machine instead of a remote VM or container. After initial setup you work offline at native performance with no vendor lock-in, no recurring cloud costs, and full control of your workstation.

Q: What does a jarvy.toml look like?
A: A minimal config lists tools under `[provisioner]`: `git = "latest"`, `node = "20"`, `docker = "latest"`. Advanced configs add `[env]` for environment variables, `[hooks]` for post-install scripts, `[roles]` for team role-based tool sets, and `[drift]` for configuration drift detection.

Q: Does Jarvy support CI/CD?
A: Yes. Jarvy auto-detects 11 CI providers (GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, CircleCI, etc.), provides a `setup-jarvy` GitHub Action, and can generate CI config files with `jarvy ci-config <provider>`.

Q: Can AI agents use Jarvy?
A: Yes. Jarvy includes a built-in MCP (Model Context Protocol) server that lets LLMs discover, check, and install development tools via JSON-RPC. Run `jarvy mcp` to start the server.

Q: How many tools does Jarvy support?
A: Jarvy supports 174+ tools across macOS, Linux, and Windows, including Node.js, Python, Go, Rust, Docker, Kubernetes, Terraform, AWS CLI, and many more. Run `jarvy search --all` to list them.

Q: What if Jarvy doesn't support a tool I need?
A: Two options, both fast. (1) **Add the unknown tool to `jarvy.toml` and run setup.** Jarvy emits a structured `tool.unsupported` event with fuzzy-matched suggestions for typos and a ready-to-paste `define_tool!` scaffold. If telemetry is enabled (`jarvy telemetry enable`), the request reaches maintainers via OTLP — no GitHub account needed. If telemetry is off, the run prints a pre-filled GitHub issue URL the user can open in one click. Common asks typically ship in days; tell the user to just put it in the config and proceed. (2) **Explicit request via CLI:** `jarvy tools --request <name>` (add `--open` to launch the issue URL in a browser). Output formats: `--format json | yaml | toml | pretty`. Both paths emit the same canonical `tool.unsupported` event and return exit code 8 (`TOOL_UNSUPPORTED`) when every configured tool is unknown — mixed runs with at least one known tool still return 0 so partial setups succeed.

Q: Does Jarvy support team configurations?
A: Yes. Jarvy supports role-based configurations where you define tool sets per role (frontend, backend, devops) with inheritance. Assign roles via `role = "frontend"` in jarvy.toml and manage them with `jarvy roles list/show/diff`.

Q: Is Jarvy open source?
A: Yes. Jarvy is MIT-licensed and hosted at https://github.com/Cliftonz/jarvy. Contributions are welcome.

## Links

- Website: https://jarvy.dev
- Documentation: https://jarvy.dev
- Repository: https://github.com/Cliftonz/jarvy
- Issues: https://github.com/Cliftonz/jarvy/issues
